hints: Track the number of links n; cutting link j (uniform in 1..n) leaves j−1 links.
So n → m where m is uniform on {0,...,n−1}: a_n = 1 + (1/n)Σ_{m=0}^{n−1} a_m.
This solves to the harmonic number a_n = H_n; with 6 links a_6 = H_6 = 49/20.
solution:
Let $a_n$ be the expected cuts with $n$ links. Cutting link $j$ (uniform on $1..n$) drops everything below, leaving $j-1$ links, so the new count is uniform on $\{0,\dots,n-1\}$:
$$a_n=1+\frac1n\sum_{m=0}^{n-1}a_m,\quad a_0=0.$$
Comparing $n\,a_n-(n-1)a_{n-1}$ gives $a_n=a_{n-1}+\tfrac1n$, i.e. $a_n=H_n$. With $6$ links, $a_6=H_6=1+\tfrac12+\tfrac13+\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac16=\tfrac{49}{20}.$

hints: Each roll is decisive only on a tie (Buster wins) or Buster-higher (Dave wins); Dave-higher re-rolls.
P(tie)=6/36, P(Buster>Dave)=15/36; condition on a decisive outcome.
solution: Per simultaneous roll: tie (Buster wins) w.p. $\tfrac{6}{36}$, Buster strictly higher (Dave wins) w.p. $\tfrac{15}{36}$, Dave strictly higher (re-roll) w.p. $\tfrac{15}{36}$. Conditioning on a decisive roll, $P(\text{Buster})=\dfrac{6/36}{6/36+15/36}=\dfrac{6}{21}=\dfrac27.$

hints: Let x be the count before; x divisible by 12, and x+50 divisible by 25.
So x ≡ 0 (mod 12) and x ≡ 0 (mod 25) ⇒ x divisible by 300; minimum x=300.
Adults before = 5x/12 = 125 (check: after, 11·350/25=154 adults, bus added 29, valid).
solution: Let $x$ be the attendees before the bus, with $\tfrac{5}{12}x$ adults, so $12\mid x$. After, $\tfrac{11}{25}(x+50)$ are adults, so $25\mid x+50$, i.e. $25\mid x$. Thus $\mathrm{lcm}(12,25)=300\mid x$; the smallest is $x=300$, giving $\tfrac5{12}\cdot300=125$ adults before. (Check: after, $\tfrac{11}{25}\cdot350=154$ adults — the bus added $29$, which is valid.) Minimum is $125.$

hints: Use strong memorylessness: P(X>A+B)=P(X>A)·P(X>B) for independent A,B.
solution:
By the strong memorylessness of the exponential, $P(X>T_1+\dots+T_{10})=\prod_{i=1}^{10}P(X>T_i)$. For each, with $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(1/90)$ and $T_i\sim\mathrm{Exp}(1/10)$ independent,
$$P(X>T_i)=\int_0^\infty e^{-t/90}\tfrac1{10}e^{-t/10}dt=\frac{1/10}{1/10+1/90}=\frac{9}{10}.$$
Thus the probability is $(9/10)^{10}$, so $q=\tfrac{9}{10},\,b=10$ and $qb=9.$

hints: How many 3-unit lengths fit along each 20-unit edge?
⌊20/3⌋ = 6 per dimension.
solution: Along each edge, $\lfloor20/3\rfloor=6$ small cubes fit. In three dimensions that is $6^3=216.$

hints: N₁ is a stopping time; apply Wald's identity to S_{N₁}.
E[S_{N₁}] = E[N₁]·E[X], with E[N₁]=e (expected draws to exceed 1) and E[X]=1/2.
solution: $N_1$ is a stopping time for the IID sequence, so by Wald's identity $\mathbb{E}[S_{N_1}]=\mathbb{E}[N_1]\,\mathbb{E}[X_1]$. The classic result $\mathbb{E}[N_1]=e$ (expected number of uniforms to first exceed $1$) together with $\mathbb{E}[X_1]=\tfrac12$ gives $\mathbb{E}[S_{N_1}]=\tfrac{e}{2}$, so $c=\tfrac12.$

hints: Abby wins on flip k if both got tails the first k−1 rounds, then Abby heads.
Deep ITM put behaves like a short position in the stock.
Each $1 drop in S raises the put ~$1.
Delta ≈ −1.
solution: A deep in-the-money put is almost certain to be exercised, so it behaves like a short stock position: a $\$1$ fall in the underlying raises its value by about $\$1$. Hence $\Delta\approx-1.$

hints: Coupon collector: sum the waits T_i for each new face.
After i−1 faces seen, a new one appears w.p. (6−(i−1))/6, so E[T_i]=6/(7−i).
solution:
This is the coupon-collector problem. After $i-1$ distinct faces, a new face appears with probability $\tfrac{6-(i-1)}{6}$, so $\mathbb{E}[T_i]=\tfrac{6}{7-i}$. Summing,
$$\mathbb{E}[T]=\sum_{i=1}^6\frac{6}{7-i}=6\Big(1+\tfrac12+\tfrac13+\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac16\Big)=6\cdot\tfrac{49}{20}=14.7.$$

hints: Each vertex is counted on two sides, so 3·17 = 45 + (sum of vertices) ⇒ vertices sum to 6 ⇒ vertices are {1,2,3}.
The remaining {4,5,6,7,8,9} fill two-per-side; each side's edge-pair must sum to 17 minus its two vertices (i.e. 12, 13, 14).
Find the pairings of {4..9} into sums {12,13,14} and arrange the vertices up to rotation.
Group the moves in pairs: a 1% drop then a 1% rise.
Each pair multiplies the price by 0.99·1.01 = 0.9999 < 1.
Repeated multiplication by <1 ⇒ price → 0.
solution: Pair each drop with the following rise: the price is multiplied by $0.99\times1.01=0.9999<1$ every two steps. Iterating, the price is $100\cdot(0.9999)^{n}\to0$. So it converges to $\$0.$

hints: Coupon collector with 4 suits: sum the waits for each new suit.
solution:
This is coupon collecting with $4$ suits. The wait for the $i$-th new suit is geometric with success probability $\tfrac{4-(i-1)}{4}$, so $\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\tfrac{4}{5-i}$:
$$\mathbb{E}[Z]=1+\tfrac43+\tfrac42+\tfrac41=1+\tfrac43+2+4=\tfrac{25}{3}.$$
